textPlain:
PHP becomes a complex language with each update. For what reason?
Its application is still limited to the web, mostly.

A lot of people are too proud to be associated with PHP.  I am ready to admit that know nothing about the language except that a lot of people make cool things with it.My favourite PHP product at the moment is BookStack (https://www.bookstackapp.com/), a really good wiki.  I run an instance for my family and it's great.But there are loads of things.  And I notice that many of the sites I like using...are built on well maintained PHP stacks.
PHP has really come a long way since I used it in the 90s on my first little website.
The most exciting thing about the PHP8.5 announcement is the stability and maturity of PHP
Looking forward for true async to land - nothing here gets me too excited.
still remember been on #php with count down to php3 announcement
tl;dr: nothing interesting, just stability and maturity
The web is not getting any simpler.

I downvoted you before reading the fine article. I'm back to correct that.The new array_first() and array_last() functions are nice, everything else is either reimplantation of existing features or "features"which will make maintainability more difficult. The pipe operator is one such example. I don't need it - these nested methods are not really an issue in any codebase I've seen. The new syntax only works for unary functions, so higher arity functions must be wrapped in an arrow function. It's a mess and more bug prone than just nesting the functions.

PHP is a very pleasant and straight-forward language to work with. I enjoyed my time working with it, though I did also see quite a lot of very poor code.I think the danger with PHP is more its ability to easily cause *very bad things*.This would partially be poor training (my University literally taught PHP with SQL-injectable examples), and I think the language itself making it very easy, such that less-experienced developers using it - most of them, early on - don't realise what's wrong until it's gone wrong.With PHP being such an early tool online, and the above properties existing, it earned a reputation for being insecure and bad.
> A lot of people are too proud to be associated with PHP.How so?

I remember the discussions at the time about which filename extension to use - and I can not believe that .php3 won. I think that I was the first to bring up the subject for PHP 4, to use .php again and not include the version number.
